title: Williams Textbook of Endocrinology
abstract:
5000 pages of manuscripts to Frank
Shepard's index of legal cases in Illinois
in 1873, Kronick has drawn from a wide
range of fascinating examples taken
from both inside and outside the life
sciences to give the reader a broad
understanding of this literature.
Ann C. Weller
University of Illinois, Chicago
Alcohol
Alcohol: Use and Abuse in America, by Jack H.
Mendelson and Nancy K. Mello, 395 pp, with illus,
$24.50, Boston, Little Brown & Co Inc, 1985.
Americans may be drinking less alco-
hol today than at any time since the
Pilgrims arrived nearly four centuries
ago. One cannot be sure because data on
alcohol consumption come from tax rec-
ords, and some consumption never gets
recorded. But probably less untaxed
alcohol (moonshine) is consumed today
than ever before, and taxed consump-
tion in the 1980s is less than half of what
it was in 1791, when alcohol was first
taxed.
Why the decline? Some Americans
prefer marijuana, and this may be one
reason. The slimness fad may be an-
other. Nobody knows, but one thing is
certain: Americans have always been
deeply involved with alcohol.
"America's ambivalence toward
alcohol.
. ."
In this richly informative book, Jack
Mendelson and Nancy Mello tell just
how deeply involved Americans
are
with drinking. First there was cider,
which was pretty tame. Then came the
distilled beverages, rum and whiskey,
and America quickly acquired a reputa-
tion as a "nation of drunkards." The
reputation persisted. Baudelaire, writ-
ing about Poe, said that Poe drank "not
as an epicure, but barbarously, with a
speed and dispatch altogether Ameri¬
can." Even in the age of California
coolers, only the Soviets have a worse
reputation for tippling (although, in
fact, the United States today ranks 18th
among nations in alcohol consumption).
Mendelson and Mello do an outstand¬
ing job of tracing America's love-hate
relationship with alcohol from colonial
times to the present. At first it was
mostly love. In 1673, the Puritan divine
Increase Mather said alcohol was "a
creature of God, to be received with
thankfulness." Later his son, Cotton,
said "the flood of excessive drinking"
was about to "drown Christianity."
America's ambivalence toward alco¬
hol has never been better described
than in this volume. In the past, even
when most people drank, Demon Rum
was seen as a terrible scourge, the cause
of most poverty and crime. Even today,
the authors say, "this simplistic scape-
goating of an intoxicant
.
.
. seems a
predictable accompaniment of social un¬
rest and economic problems."
There is more to the book than his¬
tory. It is crammed with graphs, charts,
and statistics about everything from
beer production to government regula¬
tion tothe fate ofthe alcoholmolecule on
ingestion. There are even sections on
other drugs, such
as marijuana and
cocaine, written
as expertly
as one
would expect, given the authors' long
involvement in alcohol and drug re¬
search. The authors have made impor¬
tant contributions to our knowledge of
these matters, and their book is an
admirable distillation, so to speak, of
what is known.
Donald W. Goodwin, MD
Kansas University
Medical Center
Kansas City
Virology
Fundamental Virology, edited by Bernard
N.
Fields and David M. Knipe, 768 pp, with illus,
$49.50, New York, Raven Press, 1986.
This book, published in June 1986, is
exactly what the editors describe it to
be: ".
.
. it consists of a set of chapters
excerpted from Virology [an earlier text
published in 1985]. Its goal is to provide
a text for graduate and upper level
undergraduate students, researchers,
scientists and investigators."
There are approximately one half of
the pages in the original and 33 rather
than 63 chapters, including 17 introduc-
tory chapters covering aspects such as
structure, taxonomy, viral membranes,
and viral genetics. The only topic from
the original missing here is diagnostic
virology. Many chapters are excellent;
for example, the chapter focusing on
defective genomes is as inclusive as any
I have seen on that subject. Virtually all
of the chapters from the original of clin-
ical relevance and several chapters on
uncommon virus types have not been
included. The remaining sections cover-
ing specific viruses focus on structure
and replication, with little discussion of
clinical relevance.
Because
these topics
have
been
plucked out of the original text with
rearrangement of order but not con¬
tent, there are no additions or correc¬
tions. The latest date on references is
1983. In addition, some of the chapters,
eg, orthomyxovirus and paramyxovi-
rus, refer to the chapter number from
the original text and are usually ac¬
knowledged by an editor's note at the
bottom ofthe page directing the reader
to the earlier work. Thus, the same
minor errors that existed in the original
are carried into the current volume. For
example, in the chapter on epidemiol¬
ogy, the mode ofcytomegalovirus trans¬
mission is categorized with that of mea¬
sles
and influenza.
Further,
in the
chapter
on spongiform encephalopa-
thies, the causative agent of the ac¬
quired immunodeficiency syndrome is
said to be an unknown agent or perhaps
a slow-infection-type, possibly defec¬
tive, virus.
What one does get fromthis text is an
excellent collection of chapters on gen¬
eral concepts as well as on specific vi¬
ruses and thier replication. Like most
texts in rapidly advancing fields, it is
dated by the time it is published, a fact
made worse by reprinting a year later.
However, it does provide an excellent
reference for the library of the graduate
student, the basic researcher, or even
the clinician interested in basic virology,
for a price more affordable than that of
the original.
Robert F. Betts, MD
University of Rochester Medical Center
Rochester, NY
Endocrinology
Williams Textbook of Endocrinology, edited by
Jean D. Wilson and Daniel W. Foster, ed 7; 1413 pp,
778 illus,$95, Philadelphia, WB Saunders
Co, 1985.
The great Dr Robert Hardin Williams
died in 1979. This edition of his classic
textbook is the first edited by Wilson
and Foster. In the tradition of previous
editions contributors
have been
se-
lected who are at the forefront of endo-
crinology, and each chapter is followed
by an extensive bibliography. I would
feel remiss as an endocrinologist ifI did
not buy the latest edition of this text as
soon as it became available.
However, as in previous editions, this
book occasionally suffers from
some
disorganization and at times lacks
a
proper clinical focus.
Important clinical topics are not al-
ways given the space and emphasis they
deserve. The introduction of the outpa-
tient thyroid biopsy has been one of the
most important events in thyroidology
in the last 25 years. Unbelievably, there
is only one halfpage devoted to thyroid
biopsy. The topic of hirsutism was im-
bedded in chapter 9, "Disorders of the
Ovary and Female Reproductive Tract."
The reader cannot guess the location of
this topic by examining the table of
contents or even by looking at the chap¬
ter 9 outline. Hirsutism, which is a
frequent cause ofreferrals to the clinical
endocrinologist, is covered in only two
pages.
Downloaded From: http://jama.jamanetwork.com/ by a Florida International University Medical Library User  on 06/12/2015
 Practical detail is sometimes omitted.
On page 803, in discussing the evalua¬
tion of a hot nodule, the author suggests
"a short course of suppressive therapy
followed by a suppression scan to con¬
firm that the nodule has functional au¬
tonomy." This statement is vague: How
long should the patient undergo ther¬
apy?
What preparation
and dosage
should be used? Which tracer is pre¬
ferred? Similarly, on page 850 mention
is made of the increasing problem of the
incidental finding of adrenal tumors on
computed tomographic scanning of the
abdomen. The reader is advised to "do a
battery of screening tests, previously
described in this section." A great num¬
ber of adrenal tests are, in fact, de¬
scribed in this section. What particular
tests does the author suggest? If this
text is to be useful to the student and
resident,
more clinical detail is
re¬
quired.
Occasionally there is an annoying re¬
dundancy of information. "Thyroid Bi¬
opsy" is treated on pages 738 and 802,
"Incidental Adrenal Tumors" on pages
850 and 979, and "Treatment of Prolac-
tinomas" on pages 408 and 608.
The text is at times difficult to read. I
would like to see clinical algorithms that
are easy to follow and thus beneficial to
the young clinician.
In summary, despite some shortcom¬
ings, the seventh edition of Williams
Textbook ofEndocrinology continues to
be a valuable reference for the student,
resident, and practicing endocrinolo¬
gist. The Williams textbook remains the
standard by which other endocrine
texts must be measured.
Sheldon S. Stoffer, MD
Southfield, Mich
Surgery
Basic Surgery, edited by John A. McCredie, ed 2;
656 pp, with illus, $29.95, New York, Macmillan
Publishing Co Inc, 1986.
This 656-page paperback second edi-
tion is an updated version of the original
1977 publication. As stated by the edi-
tors, it is "prepared as a textbook and
supplement for the medical student and
surgical resident."
For ease of reference the book has
been divided into four units and com-
prises 35 chapters. Unit 1 discusses
"Basic Considerations" and covers met-
abolic responses, monitoring, fluid and
electrolyte balance, immune responses,
tissue healing, surgical infections, and
cancer. The presentations are abbrevi-
ated, but for the audience intended it
serves very well. The chapters on moni-
toring and immune response are par-
ticularly well done.
Unit 2, covering "Total Care," con-
tains basic information and allows the
student to become familiar with new
terms and concepts. Units 3 and 4, on
"Principles of General Surgery" and
"Principles of Speciality Surgery," pro-
vide a general discussion of the various
body systems, emphasizing the princi-
ples of therapy rather than detailing
surgical technique. The surgical spe-
cialities are broadly discussed and pre¬
sented in
an easily read style that
should be informative for the medical
student. However, obstetrics and gyne¬
cology, ophthalmology, and otolaryngol-
ogy are not discussed. Hundreds of
illustrations add greatly to an under¬
standing of the material. All in all I
believe the authors have satisfied the
avowed purpose ofthe book: to aid medi¬
cal students.
W. G. Abel, MD
SUNY
Stony Brook, NY
Antimicrobials
The Antimicrobial Agents Annual/I, edited by
Phillip K. Peterson and Jan Verhoef (The Anti-
microbial Agents Annual, J. F. Acar et al, eds), 582
pp, with illus, $69, New York, Elsevier Science
Publishing Co Inc, 1986.
This multiauthored work (68 distin-
guished authors) consists of two major
sections. The first contains 35 chapters
dealing with antibacterial, antifungal,
antiparasitic, and antiviral agents. The
second, "Current Topics in Antimicro-
bial Therapy," consists of 12 well-writ-
ten reviews.
The editors of this ambitious work
note that it is to be the first in a series of
annual volumes designed to keep pace
with the rapid advances in antimicrobial
chemotherapy. They disclaim any re-
sponsibility in providing a comprehen-
sive textbook; instead, their goal is to
assess new information critically and to
place it in current perspective. Despite
its impressive size (582 pages), the book
may not be usable as a reference be-
cause basic information is not always
included.
The chapter on cephalosporin (San-
ders and Sanders) is relatively large (25
pages) compared with the others. A
concise overview ofnewer information is
presented with a bibliography of 150
references\p=m-\allfrom 1980 to 1985! For
some antimicrobial classes in which
there have been fewer advances, the
chapters, by and large, consist of mate¬
rial available in standard pharmacology
textbooks.
A certain unevenness and deviation
from a standard format is unavoidable in
any multiauthored text. For example,
"mechanism of action" is explicitly la¬
beled
as
a separate section in some
chapters, integrated into the introduc-
tion in other chapters, and even omitted
in a few chapters.
The chapters on quinolones, ß-lacta-
mase inhibitors, acyclovir, and ribavirin
are reasonably comprehensive, while
the chapters
on rifampin,
the
car-
bapenems, and praziquantel are disap¬
pointingly short. Monobactams are not
discussed as a group, although aztreo-
nam is covered reasonably well. The six
chapters on antiparasitic agents pro¬
vide an updated and needed review of
this group.
Despite the obvious strengths of this
section, I remain unconvinced that a
hardcover volume is an effective format
for cataloging new changes. Basic infor¬
mation on mechanisms of action, phar¬
macology, and indications is needed, if
only to provide a framework for newer
information. As a result, chapters in the
first section are a curious hodgepodge of
old and new information. I wonder, with
an upcoming Antimicrobial Agents An-
nual-II, what information in volume 1
will be retained and duplicated? The
usefulness of the first section of volume
1 will be dependent on the frequency
and the format of the succeeding vol¬
umes.
The second section dealing with cur¬
rent topics (217 pages) is the strength of
this book. It includes "Antibiotic-In¬
duced
Hemostatic
Abnormalities"
(Johnson) and "Treatment of Methicil-
lin-Resistant Staphylococci"
(Rahal).
Review topics
include
antimicrobial
therapy for acquired immunodeficiency
syndrome-related
infections
(Wofsy)
and an updated survey of antibiotic dos¬
ing in renal insufficiency (Matzke and
Keane). The concise review on antibiotic
tissue penetration (Peterson and Gerd-
ing) is particularly well done.
Overall, the book is excellent and a
desirable addition to any medical li¬
brary. It is also recommended for phar¬
macists and physicians with special in¬
terests
in
infectious
diseases
and
antibiotics.
Victor L. Yu, MD
University of Pittsburgh
Gynecology
Gynecology: Principles and Practice, by Robert
W. Kistner, ed 4; 716 pp, with illus, $65, Chicago,
Year Book Medical Publishers Inc, 1986.
Gynecology Principles and Practice,
fourth edition, by Robert W. Kistner,
MD, is a well executed textbook, which
affords the reader with a nicely bal-
anced overview of the basic science of
gynecology coupled with information on
clinical diagnosis and management.
The first half of the book is laid out by
organ system, with each chapter con-
taining subchapters on that particular
